thai: เส้นหมี่ตับห่าน

food_subgroup: Noodle dish with luxury protein (foie gras)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for foie gras vermicelli specifically. FOODON_00002187 (noodle dish) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC (Pasta and noodles, cooked) is the closest parent. No specific code exists for foie gras vermicelli or Thai foie gras noodle dishes.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for foie gras vermicelli or sufficiently close noodle-with-liver-pate dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of foie gras vermicelli is approximately 350 g, comprising a portion of pan-seared foie gras (approximately 60–80 g) served over stir-fried rice vermicelli noodles with aromatics and sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Estimated composite: USDA FoodData Central — Goose liver (pate, canned) NDB 05305; USDA FoodData Central — Rice noodles, cooked (SR Legacy 20113); FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table for Southeast Asia (2020) — rice vermicelli cooked
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for foie gras vermicelli as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite: approximately 22% foie gras (duck/goose liver pate, USDA NDB 05305) and 78% cooked rice vermicelli (USDA SR Legacy 20113 / FAO INFOODS SEA). Weighting based on typical restaurant portion composition (70 g foie gras, 280 g cooked vermicelli per 350 g serving). Sauce and aromatics contribute minor sodium and fat increments estimated from USDA condiment entries. Confidence is medium due to composite estimation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Rice noodles, cooked (GI 53, glucose reference); Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, Buyken AE, Goletzke J. Am J Clin Nutr. 2021
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No GI value exists in approved sources for foie gras vermicelli as a composite dish. GI is estimated from the dominant carbohydrate component: cooked rice noodles/vermicelli (GI 53, glucose reference) per Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. Foie gras contributes negligible carbohydrate and does not materially alter the GI. GL per serving calculated as: (GI 53 × carbohydrate per serving in grams) / 100 = (53 × 75.25) / 100 ≈ 39.9, where carbohydrate per 350 g serving = 21.5 × 3.5 = 75.25 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce, oyster sauce, and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to an estimated sodium content of approximately 420 mg per 100 g (approximately 1,470 mg per standard 350 g serving), which represents over 60% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2,000 mg; request reduced-sauce preparation or consume only a small portion.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from rice vermicelli carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should be aware that a standard 350 g serving carries a glycemic load of approximately 40 (high GL threshold), driven by the rice vermicelli base; portion reduction to 150–200 g and pairing with non-starchy vegetables can help moderate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat and cholesterol from foie gras; high sodium from sauces
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should treat this dish as an occasional indulgence only, as foie gras contributes significant saturated fat (approximately 3.1 g per 100 g) and cholesterol (approximately 95 mg per 100 g), and the high sodium load from sauces further elevates cardiovascular risk; a 350 g serving provides approximately 332.5 mg cholesterol, approaching the upper limit recommended for at-risk individuals.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Foie Gras Vermicelli is a French-Thai fusion dish that pairs pan-seared duck or goose foie gras — a luxury French delicacy made from the fattened liver of a duck or goose — with stir-fried rice vermicelli noodles seasoned with garlic, oyster sauce, soy sauce, sesame oil, and white pepper. The dish is served in upscale Thai-influenced restaurants in Singapore and Thailand, where French luxury ingredients are incorporated into Southeast Asian noodle formats. The foie gras is typically seared to a golden crust with a rich, buttery, and slightly gamey interior, while the vermicelli provides a light, silky, and mildly savoury base. The combination creates a contrast of textures: the soft, yielding liver against the delicate, slippery noodles. Nutritionally, the dish is energy-dense due to the high fat content of foie gras, which is exceptionally rich in vitamin A (retinol), vitamin B12, iron, and copper. The rice vermicelli contributes the majority of carbohydrates and has a moderate glycemic index of approximately 53. The dish is high in sodium due to the sauce components and is not suitable for individuals on low-sodium, low-cholesterol, or low-saturated-fat diets. It is not halal-certified. Clinically, it poses moderate-to-high concern for individuals with hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes (due to glycemic load), and cardiovascular disease (due to saturated fat and cholesterol from foie gras). The dish is available at premium Thai seafood restaurants in Singapore such as Shrimp Prawn Seafood at Boat Quay, priced at approximately SGD 50.80 per serving.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for foie gras vermicelli as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA FoodData Central entries for goose/duck liver pate and cooked rice noodles, with minor sauce contributions estimated from USDA condiment data. GI is estimated from the dominant carbohydrate component (rice noodles, GI 53) per Atkinson et al. 2021. Confidence is medium because the composite estimation introduces uncertainty, particularly for micronutrients and sodium from variable sauce quantities.
notes: 1. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reliably reported for this composite dish in the source databases used; foie gras contains trace vitamin D but the value is not consistently reported in USDA NDB 05305. 2. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the primary USDA source entries used for this composite estimation. 3. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy rice noodle entry (20113) and is not consistently reported for foie gras pate in NDB 05305. 4. This dish is a French-Thai fusion item served at premium Thai seafood restaurants in Singapore. The cuisine tag 'Thai' from the input is retained as the restaurant context is Thai, though the dish incorporates French luxury ingredients. 5. The dish is not halal due to the use of foie gras (duck/goose liver from force-fed animals, not halal-slaughtered). 6. Glycemic load per serving is calculated as (GI 53 × 75.25 g carbohydrate per 350 g serving) / 100 = 39.9, which falls in the high GL range (>20), indicating significant postprandial glucose impact at full serving size. 7. The food_name has been canonicalised from 'French Foie Gras Vermicelli' to 'Foie Gras Vermicelli'; the original field survey menu name is preserved in aliases as 'French Foie Gras Vermicelli'. 8. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, and water_g are estimated from the USDA composite sources described in nutrient_source.
